name: Federal Reserve
headline: Federal Reserve decisions, central banking history, debt, and the structure of monetary power.
description: This topic hub connects the origins of central banking, the mechanics of the Federal Reserve, and the current policy decisions shaping inflation, debt service, and financial instability.
metaDescription: Federal Reserve reporting and primary-source research from Veritas Worldwide. Explore central banking history, FOMC decisions, debt, inflation, and the institutions behind monetary power.

faq.question: What does Veritas cover in its Federal Reserve reporting?
faq.answer: The coverage spans the creation of central banking, the Federal Reserve's institutional design, the mechanics of money creation, and current policy decisions such as rate holds, debt-service pressure, and inflation shocks.

faq.question: How are Federal Reserve claims sourced here?
faq.answer: Priority goes to official Federal Reserve statements, congressional records, Treasury and CRS documents, court filings, and clearly labeled supporting journalism when context is needed.

faq.question: How can I follow new Federal Reserve investigations?
faq.answer: Subscribe to the free Veritas newsletter from this page and we will send new source-first reporting on central banking, debt, and monetary policy directly to your inbox.

name: Evidence Methods
headline: Public records, citation standards, source ladders, and the method behind Veritas dossiers.
description: This topic hub organizes the publication's evidence standards, public-record research paths, and source hierarchy so readers can inspect how claims move from records to analysis.
metaDescription: Evidence-methods research from Veritas Worldwide. Explore public records, FOIA, Congress.gov, FEC, SEC EDGAR, CourtListener, source hierarchy, and citation discipline.

faq.question: What is the evidence-methods hub for?
faq.answer: It gives readers and operators one place to inspect Veritas source labels, public-record workflows, dossier standards, and the boundaries between verified record, circumstantial analysis, disputed claims, and opinion.

faq.question: Does a public record prove every interpretation attached to it?
faq.answer: No. A record proves only what that record can support: a filing, disclosure, vote, docket entry, contract, archival item, or official statement. Broader conclusions must be labeled and supported by additional records.

faq.question: How should readers challenge a Veritas claim?
faq.answer: Start with the cited source, check the document date and custodian, compare it to later amendments or rulings, and separate what the source proves from what the analysis infers.

subscribeHeading: Get new source-method and dossier updates.
subscribeSubtext: Follow new evidence standards, public-record workflows, citation audits, and dossier expansions.
